sound_type: deep resonant hoot -- 'hoo-h'HOO-hoo-hoo' at 250-600 Hz fundamental, bass-register, far-carrying (audible at 1+ mile), four to five notes with measured rhythm, the quintessential owl sound, E=4 powerful but intermittent
ecosystem: Varied forest, resident year-round. Old-growth, second-growth, suburban woodlots, riparian corridors, urban parks. The most adaptable owl in North America.
season: Year-round resident. Does not migrate. Peak hooting: November-February (courtship, territory). Earliest nester in North America: eggs January-February.
family: Strigidae (STREAK BREAKER -- ends 17 consecutive Anatidae at 118-134. First new family since Cinclidae at degree 116.)

coordinates.description: Reference for urban/suburban Great Horned Owl territory. Found throughout PNW from coast to Cascades, sea level to treeline.

taxonomy_note.description: ANATIDAE STREAK BROKEN at 17 consecutive (118-134). Strigidae enters at degree 135. First new family since Cinclidae at degree 116 -- 19 degrees between new family introductions. The Great Horned Owl is the most powerful owl in North America, the 'tiger of the woods.' E=4 reflects the deep, resonant, far-carrying hoot: individually powerful but intermittent. Major ecosystem shift: water to forest, prey to predator, diurnal to nocturnal, flock to solitary, chorus to solo.
taxonomy_note.current_family: Strigidae (STREAK BREAKER -- first entry since Spotted Owl at degree 4)
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Strigidae: second entry. First was Spotted Owl (degree 4). 131 degrees between Strigidae entries.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Bubo from Latin 'eagle owl.' virginianus from Virginia, where the species was first described. Great Horned from the prominent ear tufts ('horns').
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Bubo -- first entry. The eagle owl genus. Worldwide distribution. The Great Horned Owl is the New World representative of the Bubo eagle owls.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Strigiformes. First entry since Spotted Owl (degree 4). The owl order returns after 131 degrees.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Strigidae breaks the Anatidae streak. New family for the first time in 19 degrees. Major ecosystem transition.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 250-600 Hz. Deep bass-register. Lower than most other North American owls. The low frequency travels through forest canopy with minimal attenuation.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 300-500 Hz. The resonant center of the hoot. Deep, carrying, unmistakable.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Hoot: 250-600 Hz. Alarm: higher, harsher bark. Juvenile begging: raspy scream.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Complete hoot sequence: 2-3 seconds. Individual notes: 0.3-0.6 seconds. Series of 3-8 repetitions with 10-30 second gaps.
song_metrics.note_structure: DEEP RESONANT HOOT: 'hoo-h'HOO-hoo-hoo.' Four to five notes. Third and fourth notes typically emphasized. Second note slightly stuttered. Pattern is highly stereotyped but individually variable.
song_metrics.note_types: Territorial hoot (primary, the defining sound), alarm bark (harsh, explosive), juvenile begging scream (raspy, drawn-out), bill clacking (aggression display), female hoot (higher-pitched version -- see degree 136).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Hoot series with 10-30 second gaps between sequences. The rhythm is deliberate and measured. In the pair duet (degree 136), male and female alternate in call-and-response.
song_metrics.timbre: Deep. Resonant. Bass-register. The facial disc amplifies and projects the sound forward. The large body cavity provides resonance. The result is a sound that feels larger than the bird producing it.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Individual hoot is powerful and far-carrying (1+ mile on calm nights) but intermittent. Not the sustained chorus volume of goose flocks. Powerful when it occurs, but with gaps of silence.
song_metrics.tempo: Slow. Deliberate. Hoot series at measured intervals. The pacing conveys authority -- this is not a frantic sound but a measured territorial declaration.
song_metrics.context: Territory declaration, pair communication, courtship. Year-round hooting with peak in November-February. The primary nocturnal vocalization of the PNW forest.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Subspecies vary slightly in size and coloration. Vocal variation is minimal -- the hoot pattern is largely innate and consistent across the range.
song_metrics.key_property: DEEP RESONANT HOOT -- E=4. 'Hoo-h'HOO-hoo-hoo' at 250-600 Hz. Bass-register, far-carrying (1+ mile). The quintessential owl sound. ANATIDAE STREAK BROKEN at 17 (118-134). Strigidae enters. First new family since Cinclidae (116). Tiger of the woods -- 300 psi talon grip, apex nocturnal predator, earliest nester in North America. Major ecosystem shift: water to forest, diurnal to nocturnal.

plumage.overall: Mottled brown and gray. Large body, broad wings, prominent ear tufts ('horns'). Yellow eyes. Facial disc with dark border. White throat bib. Barred underparts.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Prominent ear tufts. Yellow eyes. Large size. White throat bib. Facial disc. The combination is unmistakable -- the most recognizable owl silhouette in North America.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round plumage is similar. Regional subspecies vary in darkness (Pacific Northwest birds tend to be darker).
plumage.flight_pattern: Silent flight -- specialized feather edges break up turbulence. Broad wings, short tail. Powerful but silent. The owl can approach prey without audible warning.

physical_description.overall: Body length 46-63 cm (18-25 inches). Weight 0.9-2.5 kg (2-5.5 lbs). Wingspan 91-153 cm. Female larger than male (reversed sexual dimorphism). One of the largest owls in North America.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Ear tufts ('horns'). Yellow eyes. Large head. Barrel-shaped body. White throat bib. In silhouette, unmistakable.
physical_description.field_id: Listen for the deep hoot at night. In daytime, look for the large silhouette in evergreen trees -- ear tufts and barrel body are diagnostic. Crows and jays mobbing a large shape in a tree often leads to a roosting Great Horned Owl.

nodes.title: Anatidae Streak BROKEN at 17 (118-134)
nodes.description: Seventeen consecutive Anatidae -- the all-time record by massive margin (Picidae was 9). Six genera. Four subfamilies. Strigidae enters at degree 135. First new family since Cinclidae at degree 116 (19 degrees ago).

nodes.title: Deep Resonant Hoot -- E=4 Quintessential Owl Sound
nodes.description: 'Hoo-h'HOO-hoo-hoo' at 250-600 Hz. Bass-register, far-carrying (1+ mile). Four to five measured notes. The defining sound of the nocturnal PNW forest. Powerful but intermittent. Year-round hooting with November-February peak.

nodes.title: Tiger of the Woods -- Apex Nocturnal Predator
nodes.description: 300 psi talon grip. Most diverse diet of any NA owl (mice to raptors). Predator of predators (kills Red-tailed Hawks, Barred Owls). Takes skunks (poor sense of smell). Silent flight. Asymmetric ears for 3D sound localization.

nodes.title: Major Ecosystem Shift at Degree 135
nodes.description: Water to forest. Prey to predator. Diurnal to nocturnal. Flock to solitary. Chorus to solo. Migratory to resident. Every ecological axis shifts when Strigidae replaces Anatidae.

nodes.title: Strigidae Enters -- First New Family Since Degree 116
nodes.description: Second Strigidae after Spotted Owl (degree 4). 131 degrees between Strigidae entries. First new family introduction since Cinclidae at degree 116. The owl order returns to the engine.

cross_references.relationship: STREAK BROKEN! Ross's Goose (134, E=3, Anatidae) was the seventeenth and final Anatidae. Great Horned Owl (135, E=4, Strigidae) breaks the streak. From the smallest goose to the apex nocturnal predator.
SEVENTEEN! Harlequin (118, E=5, stream) to Ross's (134, E=3, field). The Anatidae streak ran 17 degrees. Nearly doubled the Picidae record (9). Now broken by Strigidae at degree 135.
FIRST NEW FAMILY IN 19 DEGREES! Cinclidae entered at 116. From 116 to 135 -- 19 degrees with only Anatidae between new families. The longest gap between new family introductions in the engine.
STRIGIDAE RETURNS! Spotted Owl (4, E=3) was the engine's first Strigidae. Great Horned Owl (135, E=4) is the second. 131 degrees between Strigidae entries. The owl order returns.

DOUBLE ENTRY! Great Horned Owl gets two consecutive entries: solo hoot (135, E=4) and pair duet (136, E=5). Third species with double entries after Pileated Woodpecker (108-109) and American Dipper (116-117).
